  2. Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset

Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset

Author

Shiu-Ting Ling

Handout date: Monday, March 30, 2026

FIRM DUE DATA: Saturday, April 18, 2026

Submission Format: Submit your lab as R Markdown document Lab05YourName.rmd to eLearning’s SumitLab05 link in the Labs folder. Put your name behind the author’s keyword into the header of the YAML header of the Markdown file. Make sure that your R Markdown document renders properly into an html document. Otherwise, we will not be able to grade your lab!

This lab counts 13 points towards your final grade.

Your lab answer has obtained xx out of 13 points?

Preamble

You will build a spatial regression model to analyze the 2018 American Community Survey percent of the civilian non-institutionalized population with health insurance in the census tracts of Dallas County. You need to [i] formulate common sense hypotheses, [ii] explore the data and build an appropriate regression model as well as [iii] perform proper model diagnostics, which lead to reasonably interpretable final model. Furthermore, you investigate [iv] the presence of potential heteroscedasticity and [v] a build a spatial regression model, perhaps, incorporating identified heteroscedasticity. You are expected to justify, document, interpret and/or comment each step of your analysis.

Theme Notes on Health Insurance in the U.S.A.

The civilian non-institutionalized population (CNIP) is frequently used in health insurance studies. It comprises of the population 16 years and older, who are not inmates of institutions (penal, mental facilities, homes for the aged), and who are not on active duty in the Armed Forces. Inmates of institutions as well as in the persons in the Armed Forces are by default health insured. This leaves the CNIP to choose signing up for health insurance or not. Furthermore, persons 65 and older or persons with disabilities can sign-up at minimal costs for one of Social Security’s Medicare or Medicaid health insurance plans.

Data Files and Preparation

The key variables can be found in the tractShp data object in the package TexMix. These variables are augmented by the 2018 census variables in the file HealthInsuranceDallas2018.xlsx and linked to tractShp in the data preparation section below.

Additional Variables

Note the use of upper and lower case letters in the variable names.

Name Description
TRACT Factor with the Census Tract ID
CNPop Civilian non-institutionalized population (CNIP)
CNwHI CNIP with health insurance
CNwPriHI CNIP with private health insurance
CNwPubHI CNIP with public health insurance
CNnHI CNIP without health insurance
CNnHi_19 CNIP without health insurance < 19 years old
CNwDis CNIP with disability
CN_18 CNIP < 18 years old
CNwDis_18 CNIP with disability < 18 years old
CN18_64 CNIP 18 to 64 years old
CNwDis18_64 CNIP with disability 18 to 64 years old
CN65 CNIP 65 years and older
CNwDis65 CNIP with disability 65 years and older

Data Preparation

The code chunk below outlines:

[a] The merger of two files into the SpatialPolygonDataFrame tractShp. Make sure to set the proper path to the working directory (see statement in line 11) with the file DallasHousingAdd.xlsx (imported in line 81) of this RMarkdown document.

[b] The exclusion of 2 airport tracts leaving 527 census tracts with valid civilian non-institutionalized population.

[c] The calculation of your dependent variable percent of the civilian non-institutionalized population with health insurance in the variable tractShp$PctCNwHI.

[d] The addition of row-names to tractShp@data starting with the value 1.

[e] The map pattern of the dependent variable PctCNwHI,

data("tractShp")
tractShp$TRACT <- as.numeric(as.character(tractShp$TRACT))                    # Convert factor to numeric

## Import support data
HealthInsurance <- read_excel("HealthInsuranceDallas2018.xlsx") 
HealthInsurance$TRACT <- as.numeric(as.character(HealthInsurance$TRACT))

## Merge by TRACT into tractShp
tractShp <- sp::merge(tractShp, HealthInsurance, by="TRACT")

## Exclude both airport tracts leaving 527 valid tracts
tractShp <- tractShp[tractShp$TRACT!=48113980000 & tractShp$TRACT!=48113980100, ] 

## Calculate % of CNIP with healht insurance
tractShp$PctCNwHI <- tractShp$CNwHI/tractShp$CNPop * 100

## Add row-names 1:527 to data-frame
rownames(tractShp@data) <- 1:nrow(tractShp@data)

## Map dependent variable PctCNwHI including NAs
mapColorRamp(tractShp$PctCNwHI, tractShp, breaks=8, legend.pos="bottomright",
             map.title = "Percent of CNIP with Health Insurance",
             legend.title = "Health Insurance (%)")
plot(lakesShp, col="skyblue", border="skyblue",add=TRUE)
plot(hwyShp, col="cornsilk3", lwd=3, add=TRUE)
plot(bndShp, border="black", add=TRUE)
box()
summary(tractShp)

Be careful, from the summary statistics you see that some variables still have a few NAs for the 527 remaining census tracts.

Analysis Tasks

Selection of Independent Variables (1 point)

[a] Identify 4 to 6 potential independent metric variables and one factor that you expect to influence the spatial pattern in the proportion of the civilian non-institutionalized population with health insurance. Avoid highly redundant variables.

The selected independent Variables

Five metric variables and one factor variable are selected to explain the spatial pattern of the percent of the civilian non-institutionalized population with health insurance (PctCNwHI) across Dallas County census tracts. The selected metric variables are PERCAPINC, PCTUNIVDEG, PCTUNEMP, MEDAGE, and PCTHISPAN. The selected factor variable is CITYPERI. These variables are chosen to capture the economic, demographic, and geographic dimensions of health insurance coverage, while avoiding highly redundant variables such as PCTNOHINS, which is mathematically equivalent to the complement of the dependent variable.

[b] Formulate common-sense hypotheses why and which direction, i.e., increasing or decreasing, the potential metric variables will influence the PctCNwHI and complete the table below.

Your answer goes here

The common-sense hypotheses of metric Variables

Variable Description Rational Direction
PERCAPINC Per capita income Census tracts with higher per capita income have residents who are better able to afford private health insurance premiums. Increasing
PCTUNIVDEG % population with university degree A higher share of college-educated residents is associated with greater awareness of the importance of health insurance and better access to employer-sponsored plans. Increasing
PCTUNEMP % unemployed in labor force Higher unemployment means fewer residents have access to employer-provided health insurance, reducing overall coverage rates. Decreasing
MEDAGE Median population age Older populations are more likely to qualify for Medicare or Medicaid at minimal cost, increasing the share of insured residents. Increasing
PCTHISPAN % Hispanic population A larger Hispanic population share is associated with lower insurance rates, partly due to immigration status restrictions on eligibility and language barriers in the enrollment process. Decreasing

[c] Discuss why and how you expect the selected factor will influence the PctCNwHI.

Your answer goes here

The common-sense hypotheses of factor Variables

The factor variable CITYPERI distinguishes census tracts by their location within Dallas County, including the city of Dallas, Park Cities, and the northern, eastern, southern, and western peripheral areas. It is expected that health insurance coverage rates will vary systematically across these geographic subdivisions, even after controlling for the metric variables above.

Specifically, tracts in Park Cities and northern Dallas are predominantly higher-income and predominantly white neighborhoods, and are therefore expected to show higher insurance rates. In contrast, southern and western Dallas tracts tend to have larger Hispanic and lower-income populations, suggesting lower coverage rates. The city of Dallas core may exhibit intermediate rates due to its mixed socioeconomic composition. Therefore, CITYPERI is expected to capture residual geographic variation in health insurance access that is not fully explained by the metric variables alone.

Visual Exploration and Transformation (2 points)

Explore visually the variables using either a scatter-plot matrix or box-plots:

  • Discuss the relationship between the PctCNwHI and the independent variables.
  • Discuss the relationship between the PctCNwHI and the factor variable.
  • Discuss the outstanding relationship among the independent variables.
  • Discuss the necessity of variable transformations and shift parameters with car::symbox( ) function and when appropriate with a preference of the log-transformation.
  • If necessary remove any problematic census tracts from tractShp.

Your answer goes here

Identification and Removal of Problematic Census Tracts

To identify census tracts with potentially unreliable estimates, all 527 tracts are sorted by their civilian non-institutionalized population (CNPop) in ascending order, and the 10 smallest tracts are inspected. A separate check is then performed on the smallest tract to retrieve its PctCNwHI value and geographic region.

## Check CNPop for each tract - sorted from smallest to largest (top 10)
head(tractShp@data[order(tractShp@data$CNPop), c("TRACT", "CNPop")], 10)
          TRACT CNPop
47  48113014002    62
261 48113001701   452
105 48113008603   747
48  48113013713   833
318 48113007826   987
304 48113013104  1002
306 48113007823  1030
456 48113018403  1178
107 48113003400  1245
108 48113004000  1296
## Inspect the smallest CNPop tract
tractShp@data[tractShp@data$TRACT == 48113014002, 
              c("TRACT", "SeqId", "CNPop", "PctCNwHI", "CITYPERI")]
         TRACT SeqId CNPop PctCNwHI CITYPERI
47 48113014002    47    62      100    North

The results show that tract 48113014002 (North region) has an extremely small CNPop of only 62 persons, and its corresponding PctCNwHI is 100%. This value is statistically unreliable — with such a small population base, the estimated health insurance rate is highly sensitive to even a single person changing their insurance status, and therefore cannot be considered a meaningful representation of the tract’s true coverage rate. Furthermore, a PctCNwHI of exactly 100% is a boundary value that may cause numerical issues in subsequent log-odds or regression analyses. For these reasons, tract 48113014002 is removed jointly from both the geographic layer and the attribute table of tractShp, leaving 526 census tracts for all subsequent analyses.

The second smallest tract 48113001701 (CNPop = 452) is considered acceptable and is retained in the dataset.

## Remove tract 48113014002 (SeqId=47, CNPop=62, PctCNwHI=100%)
## Reason: extremely small CNPop leads to unreliable PctCNwHI estimate
tractShp <- tractShp[tractShp$TRACT != 48113014002, ]

## Update row-names after removal
rownames(tractShp@data) <- 1:nrow(tractShp@data)

## Confirm removal
cat("Remaining tracts after removal:", nrow(tractShp@data), "\n")
Remaining tracts after removal: 526

Tract 48113014002 is removed from tractShp using row subsetting. Since tractShp is a SpatialPolygonsDataFrame object, this operation simultaneously removes the tract from both the geographic polygon layer and the attribute table, ensuring consistency between the spatial and attribute data. The row names are subsequently reset to run from 1 to 526. The cat() statement confirms that 526 census tracts remain…

Since the minimum value of `PCTUNEMP is observed to be 0, the census tracts with a zero unemployment rate are inspected to determine whether any should be removed from the analysis.

## Check tracts with PCTUNEMP = 0
tractShp@data[tractShp@data$PCTUNEMP == 0, 
              c("TRACT", "SeqId", "CNPop", "PCTUNEMP", "PctCNwHI", "CITYPERI")]
          TRACT SeqId CNPop PCTUNEMP PctCNwHI   CITYPERI
79  48113014138    80  3863        0 96.03935       West
269 48113000702   272  3070        0 92.99674 CityDallas
275 48113000300   278  4220        0 96.49289 CityDallas
303 48113013104   306  1002        0 94.11178 CityDallas

Four census tracts with PCTUNEMP = 0 are identified but retained in the dataset, as their CNPop values are sufficiently large and the offset parameter of 0.25 applied in the log transformation adequately handles the zero values.

Then, since the minimum value of PCTHISPAN is observed to be 0, the census tracts with a zero Hispanic population share are inspected to determine whether any should be removed from the analysis.

## Check tracts with PCTHISPAN = 0
tractShp@data[tractShp@data$PCTHISPAN == 0, 
              c("TRACT", "SeqId", "CNPop", "PCTHISPAN", "PctCNwHI", "CITYPERI")]
          TRACT SeqId CNPop PCTHISPAN PctCNwHI   CITYPERI
295 48113007906   298  2283         0 97.10907 CityDallas

Only one tract (SeqId = 295, TRACT = 48113007906) has PCTHISPAN = 0. This tract has a sufficiently largeCNPop of 2,283 and a `PctCNwHI of 97.1%, which is not an extreme boundary value. The zero Hispanic population share reflects the actual demographic composition of this tract and does not indicate a data quality issue. Therefore, this tract is retained in the dataset.

Symmetry Transformation Check with symbox()

## 
## Visual Exploration and Transformation
## 

## --- [Step 1] Symbox: check transformation necessity for each variable ---
layout(matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE))          # 2x3 grid for 6 plots
symbox(tractShp$PctCNwHI,   main = "PctCNwHI")   # dependent variable
symbox(tractShp$PERCAPINC,  main = "PERCAPINC")
symbox(tractShp$PCTUNIVDEG, main = "PCTUNIVDEG")
symbox(tractShp$PCTUNEMP,   main = "PCTUNEMP")
Warning in symbox.default(tractShp$PCTUNEMP, main = "PCTUNEMP"): start set to
0.2519142
symbox(tractShp$MEDAGE,     main = "MEDAGE")
symbox(tractShp$PCTHISPAN,  main = "PCTHISPAN")
Warning in symbox.default(tractShp$PCTHISPAN, main = "PCTHISPAN"): start set to
0.9370629
layout(1)                                         # reset layout

Supplementary visual representation of the histogram before and after the metric variable log transformation:

The symbox() function displays side-by-side boxplots of each variable under different Box-Cox power transformations (λ = -1, -0.5, 0, 0.5, 1), allowing visual comparison of symmetry across transformations. The most symmetric boxplot — indicated by equal whisker lengths and a centered median line — identifies the most appropriate transformation for each variable.

For the dependent variable PctCNwHI, the boxplot at λ = 1 already shows relatively balanced whiskers and a centered median. Therefore, no transformation is applied.

For PERCAPINC, the original distribution (λ = 1) shows a noticeably longer upper whisker with many high-value outliers, indicating right skewness. The log transformation (λ = 0) produces the most symmetric boxplot, so log(PERCAPINC) is applied.

For PCTUNIVDEG, the original distribution (λ = 1) is right-skewed with an extended upper whisker. The symbox plot suggests that λ = 0.5 (square root) achieves better symmetry; however, given that the log transformation allows regression coefficients to be interpreted as elasticities, log(PCTUNIVDEG) is preferred over the square root transformation.

For PCTUNEMP, the original distribution (λ = 1) is strongly right-skewed. Since the variable contains zero values (as indicated by the warning message “start set to 0.25”), a direct log transformation is not possible. An offset of 0.25 is added before the log transformation, resulting in log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25).

For MEDAGE, the boxplots across all transformations look similar with no clear improvement from any transformation. The original distribution (λ = 1) is already reasonably symmetric, so no transformation is applied.

For PCTHISPAN, although the original distribution (λ = 1) shows some right skewness, the log transformation (λ = 0) over-corrects and produces a left-skewed distribution, which is worse than the original. Therefore, no transformation is applied to PCTHISPAN.

Relationships Among Metric Variables

## --- [Step 2] Scatterplot matrix (apply transformations per symbox results) ---
## PctCNwHI : no transformation (already symmetric)
## PERCAPINC : log transformation
## PCTUNIVDEG: log transformation
## PCTUNEMP  : log transformation with offset 0.25 (contains zero values)
## MEDAGE    : no transformation (already symmetric)
## PCTHISPAN : no transformation (log over-corrects to left skew)
scatterplotMatrix(~ PctCNwHI + log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
                    log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN,
                  data = tractShp@data, diagonal = "density",
                  smooth = list(span = 0.5, lty.smooth = 1, 
                                col.smooth = "red", col.var = "red"),
                  regLine = list(col = "green"))
Warning in applyDefaults(diagonal, defaults = list(method = "adaptiveDensity"),
: unnamed diag arguments, will be ignored

The scatterplotMatrix() function displays pairwise scatter plots of all selected variables after applying the transformations determined from the symbox() analysis. Each diagonal cell shows the kernel density estimate of the variable’s distribution. The green line represents the linear regression fit, while the red smoother captures any non-linear trend. Variables that required log transformation (PERCAPINC, PCTUNIVDEG, PCTUNEMP) are entered in their transformed form, with PCTUNEMP using an offset of 0.25 to handle zero values. PctCNwHI, MEDAGE, and PCTHISPAN are kept in their original scale.

Relationship between PctCNwHI and the metric independent variables:

The scatter plot matrix reveals that all selected metric independent variables show a meaningful relationship with the health insurance coverage rate (PctCNwHI), all consistent with the hypotheses formulated in Task [1].

log(PERCAPINC) shows a positive relationship with PctCNwHI. The red smoother curves slightly away from the green regression line, suggesting a mild non-linear pattern where the rate of increase in health insurance coverage slows down at higher income levels. log(PCTUNIVDEG) also shows a positive relationship, with the smoother indicating a slight non-linearity where coverage tends to rise more steeply at higher education levels. log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) shows a negative relationship with PctCNwHI, with the smoother closely following the regression line, suggesting the relationship is approximately linear. MEDAGE shows a positive relationship with PctCNwHI, with the smoother curving slightly away from the regression line, indicating a mild non-linear trend. PCTHISPAN shows a negative relationship with PctCNwHI, with the smoother broadly following the regression line, though the scatter is relatively wide. All observed directions are consistent with the hypotheses formulated in Task [1]. The identified non-linearities will be formally tested in Task [3] using car::residualPlots().

Relationship among the metric independent variables:

A number of notable relationships are observed among the independent variables themselves. log(PERCAPINC) and log(PCTUNIVDEG) show a strong positive relationship with a noticeable non-linear pattern, indicating that higher-income tracts tend to also have a higher share of college-educated residents. Both log(PERCAPINC) and log(PCTUNIVDEG) show negative relationships with log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25), suggesting that tracts with higher income and education levels tend to have lower unemployment rates. log(PERCAPINC) and log(PCTUNIVDEG) also show negative relationships with PCTHISPAN, indicating that tracts with larger Hispanic populations tend to have lower income and education levels simultaneously. MEDAGE shows mild positive relationships with log(PERCAPINC) and log(PCTUNIVDEG), suggesting that older tracts tend to be slightly wealthier and more educated. The relationship between log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) and PCTHISPAN appears weak with no clear linear trend. These observed correlations among independent variables raise potential multicollinearity concerns, which will be formally examined using car::vif() in Task [3].

Relationships Between Factor and Metric Variables

## Effect of CITYPERI on metric independent variables (visual)
## Reference: Chapt03a.r - boxplot(water81~retire, data=concord)

opar <- par(mfrow=c(2,3))                          # 2x3 grid for 6 variables

boxplot(PctCNwHI ~ CITYPERI, data=tractShp@data,
        cex.axis=0.7, las=2,
        main="PctCNwHI by CITYPERI")

boxplot(log(PERCAPINC) ~ CITYPERI, data=tractShp@data,
        cex.axis=0.7, las=2,
        main="log(PERCAPINC) by CITYPERI")

boxplot(log(PCTUNIVDEG) ~ CITYPERI, data=tractShp@data,
        cex.axis=0.7, las=2,
        main="log(PCTUNIVDEG) by CITYPERI")

boxplot(log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) ~ CITYPERI, data=tractShp@data,
        cex.axis=0.7, las=2,
        main="log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) by CITYPERI")

boxplot(MEDAGE ~ CITYPERI, data=tractShp@data,
        cex.axis=0.7, las=2,
        main="MEDAGE by CITYPERI")

boxplot(PCTHISPAN ~ CITYPERI, data=tractShp@data,
        cex.axis=0.7, las=2,
        main="PCTHISPAN by CITYPERI")
par(opar)                                          # reset layout

The side-by-side boxplots are produced using boxplot() with par(mfrow=c(2,3)) to display all five metric variables against CITYPERI in a single layout. This follows the approach in Chapt03a.r to visually assess whether the factor variable is systematically associated with the metric independent variables. The multivariate regression lm(cbind(...) ~ CITYPERI) is also estimated to formally test these associations.

Relationship between PctCNwHI and CITYPERI:

The boxplot of PctCNwHI by CITYPERI reveals a clear systematic variation in health insurance rates across the different geographic subdivisions of Dallas County. ParkCities exhibits by far the highest median insurance rate (approximately 97%) with an extremely narrow interquartile range, indicating consistently high coverage throughout this area. South and North follow with medians around 87% and 86% respectively. East shows a moderate median of approximately 79% with moderate spread. West and CityDallas display the lowest median rates at approximately 77%, with considerably wider spread, suggesting greater variability in insurance coverage within these areas. Overall, this pattern is consistent with the hypotheses formulated in Task [1], where wealthier suburban areas such as ParkCities were expected to have higher coverage rates, while inner-city and lower-income areas were expected to show lower rates. The wide interquartile range of CityDallas further reflects its socioeconomically diverse composition.

Relationship between CITYPERI and the metric independent variables:

The boxplots reveal that ParkCities stands out consistently across almost all metric variables. It shows by far the highest median log(PERCAPINC) (approximately 11.7), the highest median log(PCTUNIVDEG), and the highest median MEDAGE, while simultaneously exhibiting the lowest PCTHISPAN and the lowest log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25). This indicates that ParkCities is characterized by high income, high education, an older population, low unemployment, and a predominantly non-Hispanic composition — all at the same time. Among the remaining regions, North shows a slightly higher log(PERCAPINC) and log(PCTUNIVDEG) compared to other non-ParkCities areas. CityDallas and West exhibit the widest interquartile ranges across multiple variables, reflecting greater internal socioeconomic heterogeneity within these regions. These systematic differences between CITYPERI levels and the metric variables suggest potential overlap in the variation captured by the factor and the metric predictors, which may contribute to multicollinearity in the regression model. This will be formally assessed using car::vif() in Task [3].

Preliminary Regression Model and Diagnostics (3 points)

Build a preliminary regression model and perform a proper model diagnostics:

  • Signs and significance of the independent variables and \(R^2\) with and without the factor variable.
  • Partial \(F\)-test for the factors. Does the factor mask the importance of the metric independent variables or is insignificant and thus should be dropped?
  • Multicollinearity investigation with car::vif( ).
  • Non-linearity and quadratic term with car::residualPlots( ).
  • Normality of the regression residuals with car::qqPlot( ).
  • Influential cases investigation with car::influenceIndexPlot( ).
  • Do extreme observations require special handling?

Your answer goes here

Signs, Significance, and R² (with and without factor)

## ---[Step 1] Build a full model including factors---
mod.full <- lm(PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
                           log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN + CITYPERI,
               data = tractShp@data)
summary(mod.full)

Call:
lm(formula = PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + log(PCTUNEMP + 
    0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN + CITYPERI, data = tractShp@data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-23.4370  -3.1699   0.3983   3.4165  17.5218 

Coefficients:
                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)           4.71859   10.06377   0.469 0.639362    
log(PERCAPINC)        7.55833    1.13195   6.677 6.32e-11 ***
log(PCTUNIVDEG)       1.03234    0.61599   1.676 0.094362 .  
log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) -0.51764    0.48940  -1.058 0.290694    
MEDAGE                0.03320    0.05502   0.603 0.546469    
PCTHISPAN            -0.18332    0.01662 -11.031  < 2e-16 ***
CITYPERIEast          2.36921    0.77732   3.048 0.002423 ** 
CITYPERINorth         2.00326    0.93587   2.141 0.032781 *  
CITYPERIParkCities    0.60070    2.23661   0.269 0.788363    
CITYPERISouth         7.06069    1.11603   6.327 5.45e-10 ***
CITYPERIWest          2.85869    0.76099   3.757 0.000192 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 5.873 on 515 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7452,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7402 
F-statistic: 150.6 on 10 and 515 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
## Model without factor (for partial F-test)
mod.metric <- lm(PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
                              log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN,
                 data = tractShp@data)
summary(mod.metric)

Call:
lm(formula = PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + log(PCTUNEMP + 
    0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN, data = tractShp@data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-24.6947  -3.2542   0.5377   3.7448  18.8894 

Coefficients:
                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)          19.11893    9.76146   1.959  0.05069 .  
log(PERCAPINC)        5.99268    1.09088   5.493 6.18e-08 ***
log(PCTUNIVDEG)       1.68991    0.61081   2.767  0.00586 ** 
log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) -0.45322    0.50218  -0.902  0.36721    
MEDAGE                0.06775    0.05572   1.216  0.22459    
PCTHISPAN            -0.19228    0.01696 -11.339  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 6.127 on 520 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:   0.72, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7173 
F-statistic: 267.4 on 5 and 520 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

The full model mod.full (including CITYPERI) achieves an Adjusted R² of 0.7402,explaining approximately 74% of the variation in PctCNwHI across the 526 census tracts. In comparison, the metric-only model mod.metric (excluding CITYPERI) achieves a lower Adjusted R² of 0.7173. The addition of the factor variable CITYPERI increases the Adjusted R² by approximately 2.3 percentage points, indicating that the geographic subdivision captures additional variation in health insurance coverage beyond what the metric variables alone can explain.

Regarding the signs of the coefficients, log(PERCAPINC) carries a positive coefficient (7.558, p < 0.001), consistent with the hypothesis that higher income tracts have greater insurance coverage. PCTHISPAN carries a negative coefficient (-0.183, p < 0.001), consistent with the hypothesis that a larger Hispanic population share is associated with lower coverage rates. These two variables are the only metric variables that remain statistically significant in mod.full after controlling for CITYPERI.

Notably, log(PCTUNIVDEG) is significant (p = 0.006) in the metric-only model mod.metric but becomes only marginally significant (p = 0.094) in mod.full, suggesting that CITYPERI partially absorbs the variation attributed to education. log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) and MEDAGE are not significant in either model, which may indicate that their effects are already captured by other variables in the model or that their relationships with PctCNwHI are not linear after transformation.

Among the CITYPERI factor levels, CITYPERISouth (7.061, p < 0.001), CITYPERIEast (2.369, p = 0.002), CITYPERIWest (2.859, p < 0.001), and CITYPERINorth (2.003, p = 0.033) are all significantly higher than the baseline level CityDallas, suggesting that census tracts outside the core city of Dallas tend to have higher health insurance rates even after controlling for the metric variables. CITYPERIParkCities is not significant (p = 0.788), which may be because its characteristically high income and education levels are already fully captured by log(PERCAPINC) and log(PCTUNIVDEG).

Partial F-test for CITYPERI

## --- [Step 2] Partial F-test for CITYPERI ---
anova(mod.metric, mod.full)   # H0: all CITYPERI levels = 0
Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + log(PCTUNEMP + 
    0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN
Model 2: PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + log(PCTUNEMP + 
    0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN + CITYPERI
  Res.Df   RSS Df Sum of Sq      F    Pr(>F)    
1    520 19518                                  
2    515 17762  5      1756 10.183 2.565e-09 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

The partial F-test comparing mod.metric and mod.full yields F(5, 515) = 10.183 with p = 2.565e-09 (***), strongly rejecting the null hypothesis that all five CITYPERI coefficient levels are simultaneously equal to zero. Therefore, CITYPERI as a whole contributes significantly to explaining the variation in PctCNwHI and should be retained in the model.

Furthermore, a comparison of the metric-only model mod.metric and the full model mod.full shows that CITYPERI does not appear to mask the importance of the metric variables — log(PERCAPINC) and PCTHISPAN remain strongly significant in both models. The main effect is that CITYPERI partially absorbs the contribution of log(PCTUNIVDEG), reducing its significance from p = 0.006 to p = 0.094. This suggests a partial overlap between geographic location and education level, which was anticipated from the boxplot analysis in Task [2].

Multicollinearity (car::vif())

## --- [Step 3] VIF for multicollinearity ---
car::vif(mod.full)
                         GVIF Df GVIF^(1/(2*Df))
log(PERCAPINC)       7.648521  1        2.765596
log(PCTUNIVDEG)      5.521421  1        2.349770
log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) 1.553948  1        1.246575
MEDAGE               2.113854  1        1.453910
PCTHISPAN            2.594386  1        1.610710
CITYPERI             1.388443  5        1.033363

The variance inflation factors for mod.full indicate that log(PERCAPINC) (GVIF^(1/(2*Df)) = 2.766) and log(PCTUNIVDEG) (GVIF^(1/(2*Df)) = 2.350) show the highest inflation among the metric variables, which is consistent with the strong positive correlation between income and education observed in the scatterplot matrix in Task [2]. The remaining variables show GVIF^(1/(2*Df)) values below 1.7, and the factor CITYPERI shows a GVIF^(1/(2*Df)) of 1.033, indicating negligible inflation. Overall, multicollinearity appears to have only a modest impact on the estimates of mod.full — all regression coefficients carry the theoretically expected signs, and the statistically significant predictors remain significant. Therefore, multicollinearity does not pose a serious threat to the stability of the coefficient estimates in mod.full.

Non-linearity (car::residualPlots())

## --- [Step 4] Non-linearity check ---
car::residualPlots(mod.full, fitted=FALSE, layout=c(3,2))
                     Test stat Pr(>|Test stat|)   
log(PERCAPINC)         -1.1698         0.242632   
log(PCTUNIVDEG)         1.2422         0.214725   
log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25)    0.8662         0.386801   
MEDAGE                  0.2814         0.778490   
PCTHISPAN               3.2709         0.001144 **
CITYPERI                                          
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

The residualPlots() output tests for a significant Tukey quadratic term for each predictor. Among all variables, only PCTHISPAN shows a statistically significant non-linear pattern (Test stat = 3.271, p = 0.001). Inspecting the corresponding scatter plot of Pearson residuals against PCTHISPAN in Image 1, a slight upward curvature is visible at higher values of PCTHISPAN, confirming that the relationship between Hispanic population share and health insurance coverage is not fully captured by a linear term alone. Therefore, a quadratic term I(PCTHISPAN^2) will be added to the model to account for this non-linearity.

All other variables — log(PERCAPINC), log(PCTUNIVDEG), log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25), and MEDAGE — show no significant non-linearity (all p > 0.05), and their residual plots show no systematic patterns. CITYPERI has no test statistic as it is a factor variable.

Augmented Model with I(PCTHISPAN^2)

## --- [Step 4b] Augmented model with quadratic PCTHISPAN ---
mod.full2 <- lm(PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
                             log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) + MEDAGE + 
                             PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI,
                data = tractShp@data)
summary(mod.full2)

Call:
lm(formula = PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + log(PCTUNEMP + 
    0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI, data = tractShp@data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-23.4047  -3.2538   0.2721   3.4557  18.1599 

Coefficients:
                       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)          17.5174724 10.7106867   1.636 0.102554    
log(PERCAPINC)        6.3659581  1.1792011   5.399 1.03e-07 ***
log(PCTUNIVDEG)       1.5955179  0.6340899   2.516 0.012165 *  
log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) -0.4520360  0.4852751  -0.932 0.352030    
MEDAGE                0.0281098  0.0545284   0.516 0.606420    
PCTHISPAN            -0.3365367  0.0496521  -6.778 3.35e-11 ***
I(PCTHISPAN^2)        0.0016802  0.0005137   3.271 0.001144 ** 
CITYPERIEast          2.9432939  0.7898470   3.726 0.000216 ***
CITYPERINorth         1.8618244  0.9281914   2.006 0.045394 *  
CITYPERIParkCities   -0.1082730  2.2264172  -0.049 0.961232    
CITYPERISouth         7.2108062  1.1066215   6.516 1.72e-10 ***
CITYPERIWest          2.8863382  0.7539721   3.828 0.000145 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 5.818 on 514 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7504,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.745 
F-statistic: 140.5 on 11 and 514 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

The augmented model mod.full2 incorporates the quadratic term I(PCTHISPAN^2) identified as significant in the residualPlots() analysis. The results confirm that this addition is statistically justified and improves the model fit.

The quadratic term I(PCTHISPAN^2) is significant (coeff = 0.00168, p = 0.001), which is consistent with the visual evidence from the residual plot in Step 4. The combination of a negative linear coefficient for PCTHISPAN (-0.337) and a positive quadratic coefficient (0.00168) implies a concave-up (U-shaped) relationship: as the Hispanic population share increases from low to moderate levels, the health insurance rate decreases at a diminishing rate, and the decline slows down — or slightly reverses — at very high Hispanic concentration tracts. This pattern is substantively plausible: in neighborhoods where nearly the entire population is Hispanic, community-based outreach programs and Medicaid enrollment may partially offset the structural barriers to insurance coverage.

The Adjusted R² increases slightly from 0.7402 (mod.full) to 0.7450 (mod.full2), and the Residual Standard Error decreases from 5.873 to 5.818, indicating a modest but genuine improvement in model fit.

Notably, with the inclusion of I(PCTHISPAN^2), the education variable log(PCTUNIVDEG) recovers full significance (p = 0.012), suggesting that its previous marginal significance in mod.full was partly a result of confounding with the non-linear Hispanic population effect. log(PERCAPINC) (p < 0.001) and PCTHISPAN (p < 0.001) remain the dominant metric predictors. log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) and MEDAGE remain non-significant in the augmented model, which may suggest that their contributions are already captured by the combination of income, education, and geographic location. CITYPERIParkCities continues to be non-significant (p = 0.961), reinforcing the earlier interpretation that its distinctive socioeconomic profile is fully absorbed by the metric predictors. Therefore, mod.full2 is adopted as the final OLS model and will serve as the basis for the heteroscedasticity investigation in Task [4].

Normality of residuals (car::qqPlot())

## --- [Step 5] Normality of residuals ---
car::qqPlot(mod.full2, id=list(n=3, labels=rownames(tractShp@data)))
[1]  49  90 340

The Q-Q plot of the studentized residuals from the augmented model mod.full2 shows that the central bulk of the residuals follows the theoretical t-distribution closely. The vast majority of observations align along the diagonal reference line and fall within the 95% confidence envelope, indicating that the normality assumption is broadly satisfied for the main body of the data.

However, the lower left tail shows a noticeable departure from normality. Tracts 90, 49, and 340 fall distinctly outside the confidence band with studentized residuals of approximately -4.3, -3.7, and -3.5, respectively. These tracts are more severely under-predicted than expected under strict normality, indicating that their actual PctCNwHI values are considerably lower than what the model predicts given their covariate configurations. The upper tail, by contrast, shows no meaningful departure from normality.

Influential cases (car::influenceIndexPlot())

## --- [Step 6] Influential cases for augmented model ---
car::influenceIndexPlot(mod.full2, id=list(n=3, labels=rownames(tractShp@data)))

The influence index plot for the augmented model mod.full2 — which incorporates the quadratic term I(PCTHISPAN^2) — reveals a pattern largely consistent with the preliminary model mod.full, confirming that the addition of the quadratic term did not fundamentally alter the influence structure of the dataset.

Regarding Cook’s distance, tracts 49, 92, and 291 again exhibit the highest values (all approximately 0.020), indicating these tracts exert the greatest overall influence on the model estimates. However, all values remain small in absolute terms, and no tract appears to be disproportionately controlling the regression fit.

Regarding studentized residuals, tracts 90 (approximately -4.3), 340 (approximately -3.7), and 49 (approximately -3.5) show the most extreme negative values. These tracts are substantially over-predicted by the model — their actual PctCNwHI values are considerably lower than what the model expects given their predictor configurations.

Critically, the Bonferroni-corrected p-values confirm that none of these tracts constitutes a statistically significant outlier. Tract 90 shows the lowest Bonferroni p-value (approximately 0.05), which is borderline but does not clearly cross the significance threshold after correction for 526 simultaneous tests. Tracts 49 and 340 show Bonferroni p-values of approximately 0.10 and 0.70, respectively — neither is statistically significant.

Regarding hat-values (leverage), tracts 291, 244, and 243 again exhibit the highest values (approximately 0.15, 0.13, and 0.12, respectively), indicating they occupy unusual positions in the predictor space. Notably, tract 291 combines a moderately elevated Cook’s distance with a high hat-value, suggesting it is both a leverage point and exerts some influence on the coefficient estimates. Nevertheless, its Bonferroni p-value is not significant, so no removal is warranted.

It is worth noting that the tracts flagged by high hat-values (291, 244, 243) are distinct from those flagged by large studentized residuals (49, 90, 340). The former are leverage points with unusual predictor combinations but well-fitted responses, while the latter are tracts whose actual PctCNwHI values are poorly predicted by the model. Neither group qualifies as a statistically significant outlier under the Bonferroni correction, and therefore no tracts are removed from the dataset.

Handling extreme observations

The three census tracts flagged in the Q-Q plot and influence index plot — tracts 49 (TRACT 48113009610), 90 (TRACT 48113007202), and 340 (TRACT 48113013621) — are all located in CityDallas and show large negative studentized residuals, meaning their actual PctCNwHI values are substantially lower than what mod.full2 predicts.

## --- [Step 7] Inspect extreme tracts 49, 90, 340 ---
tractShp@data[c(49, 90, 340), 
              c("TRACT", "CNPop", "PctCNwHI", 
                "PERCAPINC", "PCTUNIVDEG", 
                "PCTUNEMP", "MEDAGE", 
                "PCTHISPAN", "CITYPERI")]
          TRACT CNPop PctCNwHI PERCAPINC PCTUNIVDEG PCTUNEMP MEDAGE PCTHISPAN
49  48113009610  4956 43.05892     13089          3 2.499094   28.5  68.25529
90  48113007202  8285 42.45021     13334          7 5.502670   27.6  68.95343
340 48113013621  4015 60.87173     43573         41 5.386251   37.8  33.46117
      CITYPERI
49  CityDallas
90  CityDallas
340 CityDallas

Tracts 49 and 90 share a similar data constellation: extremely low per capita income (~$13,000), very low university degree attainment (3% and 7% respectively), young median age (28–29 years), and a very high Hispanic population share (~68–69%). Although the model already accounts for the negative effect of a large Hispanic population share through the PCTHISPAN and I(PCTHISPAN^2) terms, the actual insurance rates of 43.1% and 42.5% are still far below the model’s predictions. This suggests that in highly concentrated Hispanic inner-city tracts, additional structural barriers — such as immigration status restrictions on Medicaid eligibility, language barriers in enrollment, and high rates of informal employment without employer-sponsored insurance — suppress coverage rates beyond what the selected predictors can capture.

Tract 340, by contrast, represents a distinctly different data constellation: relatively high per capita income ($43,573), substantial university degree attainment (41%), and a moderate Hispanic share (33.5%). The model predicts a high insurance rate based on its favorable socioeconomic profile, yet the actual PctCNwHI of only 60.9% is considerably lower. This may reflect a tract where a segment of higher-earning Hispanic residents — possibly self-employed individuals or undocumented immigrants not eligible for employer-sponsored or public insurance — depresses the coverage rate despite the otherwise favorable income and education levels.

To formally evaluate whether any of these three tracts qualifies as a statistically significant outlier, car::outlierTest() is applied. This function identifies the observation with the largest absolute studentized residual and reports a Bonferroni-adjusted p-value that accounts for simultaneously testing all 526 tracts.

## --- [Step 8] Formal outlier test with Bonferroni correction ---
car::outlierTest(mod.full2, n.max = 5)
    rstudent unadjusted p-value Bonferroni p
90 -4.098857         4.8258e-05     0.025384

The outlierTest() result identifies tract 90 as a Bonferroni-significant outlier with a studentized residual of -4.099 and a Bonferroni-adjusted p-value of 0.025, which falls below the conventional 0.05 threshold. Tracts 49 and 340, despite appearing prominent in the Q-Q plot and influence index plot, are not listed in the output — this indicates that their Bonferroni-adjusted p-values exceed 1 and they are therefore not statistically significant outliers after correction.

Following the outlier treatment logic demonstrated in VacModelTX_trans.rmd, tract 90 is removed from the dataset, and the augmented model mod.full2 is refitted on the remaining 525 census tracts. The outlierTest() is then re-applied to verify that no additional tracts become Bonferroni-significant once the most extreme observation has been removed.

## --- [Step 9] Remove Bonferroni-significant outlier tract 90 ---
## Reason: studentized residual = -4.099, Bonferroni p = 0.025 (< 0.05)
tractShp <- tractShp[-90, ]

## Update row-names after removal
rownames(tractShp@data) <- 1:nrow(tractShp@data)

## Refit the augmented model on the 525 remaining tracts
mod.full2 <- lm(PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
                             log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) + MEDAGE + 
                             PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI,
                data = tractShp@data)
summary(mod.full2)

Call:
lm(formula = PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + log(PCTUNEMP + 
    0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI, data = tractShp@data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-21.9482  -3.2559   0.2896   3.3958  18.1431 

Coefficients:
                      Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)          18.379247  10.551856   1.742 0.082142 .  
log(PERCAPINC)        6.291630   1.161625   5.416 9.37e-08 ***
log(PCTUNIVDEG)       1.606031   0.624568   2.571 0.010409 *  
log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) -0.489934   0.478073  -1.025 0.305936    
MEDAGE                0.026711   0.053710   0.497 0.619173    
PCTHISPAN            -0.334852   0.048908  -6.847 2.17e-11 ***
I(PCTHISPAN^2)        0.001677   0.000506   3.314 0.000985 ***
CITYPERIEast          2.849661   0.778315   3.661 0.000277 ***
CITYPERINorth         1.788843   0.914419   1.956 0.050977 .  
CITYPERIParkCities   -0.061698   2.192995  -0.028 0.977566    
CITYPERISouth         7.151633   1.090090   6.561 1.31e-10 ***
CITYPERIWest          2.782995   0.743072   3.745 0.000201 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 5.731 on 513 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7534,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7482 
F-statistic: 142.5 on 11 and 513 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
## Re-check for any remaining Bonferroni-significant outliers
car::outlierTest(mod.full2, n.max = 5)
No Studentized residuals with Bonferroni p < 0.05
Largest |rstudent|:
    rstudent unadjusted p-value Bonferroni p
49 -3.921395         0.00010003     0.052514

After removing tract 90, the refitted mod.full2 shows no remaining Bonferroni-significant outliers (outlierTest() reports “No Studentized residuals with Bonferroni p < 0.05”). The sequential outlier treatment is therefore concluded after a single removal.

A comparison of the model before and after removal confirms that the regression structure is stable: all coefficient signs, significance levels, and magnitudes remain essentially unchanged. log(PERCAPINC) (6.292, p < 0.001), PCTHISPAN (-0.335, p < 0.001), and I(PCTHISPAN^2) (0.00168, p < 0.001) continue to dominate the explanation, with the quadratic term’s significance actually strengthening slightly. Tracts 49 and 340, although visually prominent in the earlier diagnostic plots, are retained because their Bonferroni-adjusted p-values exceed the 0.05 threshold.

The model fit improves modestly: Adjusted R² rises from 0.7450 to 0.7482, and the residual standard error decreases from 5.818 to 5.731. This is consistent with the expectation that tract 90 was an isolated extreme observation rather than a symptom of deeper model misspecification. The final OLS model based on 525 census tracts will serve as the basis for the heteroscedasticity investigation in Task [4] and the spatial autocorrelation analysis in Task [5].

Revised Final Regression Model Specification (1.5 points)

Based on what you have learnt in the previous task adjust your regression model and properly interpret your final model in the light of the formulated direction of influence in the section on the selection of independent variables:

  • Signs and significance of the regression coefficients \(\beta\) and \(R^2\),
    • perhaps as elasticity and with
    • conditional effects plots to visualize any non-linearities with effects::allEffects( ).

Your answer goes here

Model Adjustment: Dropping Non-significant Variables

## --- [Step 1] Drop non-significant metric variables ---
mod.final <- update(mod.full2, . ~ . - log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) - MEDAGE)
summary(mod.final)

Call:
lm(formula = PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + PCTHISPAN + 
    I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI, data = tractShp@data)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-21.674  -3.244   0.279   3.498  18.004 

Coefficients:
                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)        12.229159   8.911618   1.372 0.170576    
log(PERCAPINC)      6.953502   0.952822   7.298 1.11e-12 ***
log(PCTUNIVDEG)     1.462455   0.601832   2.430 0.015439 *  
PCTHISPAN          -0.337691   0.048565  -6.953 1.09e-11 ***
I(PCTHISPAN^2)      0.001703   0.000505   3.372 0.000803 ***
CITYPERIEast        2.860558   0.776005   3.686 0.000252 ***
CITYPERINorth       1.917192   0.899076   2.132 0.033446 *  
CITYPERIParkCities -0.435585   2.157501  -0.202 0.840080    
CITYPERISouth       7.123980   1.082348   6.582 1.15e-10 ***
CITYPERIWest        2.837993   0.739849   3.836 0.000141 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 5.726 on 515 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7529,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7485 
F-statistic: 174.3 on 9 and 515 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
## --- [Step 2] Partial F-test: verify drop is justified ---
## H0: beta_PCTUNEMP = beta_MEDAGE = 0
anova(mod.final, mod.full2)
Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + 
    CITYPERI
Model 2: PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + log(PCTUNEMP + 
    0.25) + MEDAGE + PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI
  Res.Df   RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F)
1    515 16887                           
2    513 16848  2    39.232 0.5973 0.5507

In the previous task, the augmented model mod.full2 revealed that log(PCTUNEMP + 0.25) (p = 0.306) and MEDAGE (p = 0.619) are both statistically insignificant. Following the parsimony principle and the model adjustment pattern demonstrated in ConcordH2O.R, these two variables are dropped, and a partial F-test is applied to formally verify that their joint contribution to the model is negligible.

The partial F-test yields F(2, 513) = 0.597 with p = 0.551, which strongly accepts the null hypothesis that both dropped variables jointly have zero effect on PctCNwHI. This confirms that the reduced model mod.final is the preferred specification. Furthermore, the Adjusted R² actually rises slightly from 0.7482 in mod.full2 to 0.7485 in mod.final, and the residual standard error drops marginally from 5.731 to 5.726 — both consistent with the principle of parsimony that penalizes retaining uninformative predictors.

Interpretation of the Final OLS Model

Signs, significance, and R²

All retained regression coefficients in mod.final carry the expected signs formulated in Task [1], and all metric predictors are statistically significant:

  • log(PERCAPINC) = 6.954 (p < 0.001), per capita income is the dominant economic driver of health insurance coverage. Since the dependent variable PctCNwHI is in its original percentage scale while the regressor is log-transformed, the coefficient has a level-log (semi-elasticity) interpretation: a 1% increase in PERCAPINC is associated with an increase in PctCNwHI of approximately 6.954/100 ≈ 0.070 percentage points. Equivalently, a 10% increase in income raises the expected coverage rate by roughly 0.66 percentage points, and doubling a tract’s per capita income raises the expected rate by approximately 4.82 percentage points.

  • log(PCTUNIVDEG) = 1.462 (p = 0.015), the share of college-educated residents also has a positive and significant effect, consistent with the hypothesis that higher educational attainment leads to greater awareness of and access to health insurance. A 10% increase in PCTUNIVDEG is associated with approximately 0.14 percentage points increase in PctCNwHI — notably smaller in magnitude than the income effect.

  • PCTHISPAN = -0.338 (p < 0.001) combined with I(PCTHISPAN^2) = 0.00170 (p < 0.001) describes a concave-up (U-shaped) relationship. The turning point lies at 0.338 / (2 × 0.00170) ≈ 99.3%, which falls outside the effective range of the data (max observed ≈ 94%). Consequently, PctCNwHI decreases monotonically across the observed range of PCTHISPAN, but the rate of decline slows down as the Hispanic share approaches very high levels.

  • The factor CITYPERI continues to contribute significantly to the model. Compared to the baseline level CityDallas, census tracts in South (+7.124, p < 0.001), East (+2.861, p < 0.001), West (+2.838, p < 0.001), and North (+1.917, p = 0.033) all show higher expected PctCNwHI. ParkCities is essentially indistinguishable from CityDallas (-0.436, p = 0.840), which makes sense because its distinctive socioeconomic profile (very high income, high education, older population) is already fully absorbed by log(PERCAPINC) and log(PCTUNIVDEG) in the model. Despite this non-significant single level, the factor as a whole was shown to be highly significant in the partial F-test of Task [3], and is therefore retained in its entirety following the rule that a factor must be either kept or dropped as a unit.

The final model based on 525 census tracts achieves an Adjusted R² of 0.7485, explaining approximately 75% of the spatial variation in the percent of the civilian non-institutionalized population with health insurance across Dallas County. mod.final will serve as the OLS baseline for the heteroscedasticity investigation in Task [5].

Conditional Effects Plots Interpretation

## --- [Step 3] Conditional effects plots ---
effFinal <- effects::allEffects(mod.final)
plot(effFinal)

The effects::allEffects() conditional effects plots visualize the partial relationship between PctCNwHI and each predictor, with all other variables held at their sample means. This restores the original, un-transformed scale of each variable, which makes the non-linearities introduced by the log transformations and the quadratic term much easier to interpret intuitively.

  • PERCAPINC: the fitted curve rises in a concave-down fashion from approximately 70% at $15,000 per capita income to approximately 93% at $200,000 — steep at low income levels and gradually flattening at higher incomes. This diminishing-returns pattern is the characteristic shape of a log-linear (semi-elasticity) relationship, and confirms that per capita income is the dominant economic driver of health insurance coverage.

  • PCTUNIVDEG: a similar concave-down (diminishing returns) curve, rising steeply at low education levels and gradually flattening at higher values, but with a noticeably gentler overall slope (approximately a 7 percentage point increase across the full range of education), consistent with the smaller regression coefficient of 1.46 compared to 6.95 for income.

  • PCTHISPAN: the plot appears twice because allEffects() treats PCTHISPAN and I(PCTHISPAN^2) as two separate predictor terms; both plots display the same combined linear + quadratic effect. The curve shows a clear downward trend from approximately 91% at 0% Hispanic share to approximately 74% at 90% Hispanic share, with a slight concave-up flattening at very high concentrations — visually confirming the U-shaped relationship implied by the quadratic coefficient.

  • CITYPERI: South stands out with the highest predicted rate of approximately 86%, East, North, and West cluster around 81–82%, and CityDallas and ParkCities sit at the bottom with nearly identical predicted rates of approximately 79%. This pattern directly visualizes why the CITYPERIParkCities coefficient is not significant — after controlling for income and education, Park Cities and CityDallas are indistinguishable. The wider confidence interval around ParkCities also reflects its small sample size (only 8 tracts in the dataset).

All observed patterns in the conditional effects plots are consistent with the hypotheses formulated in Task [1]. mod.final is therefore adopted as the final OLS specification and will serve as the baseline for the heteroscedasticity investigation in Task [5].

Heteroscedasticity Investigation (1.5 points)

  • Justify your selection of the weights variable.
  • Estimate the model \(\sigma_i^2=exp(\gamma_0\cdot1+\gamma_1\cdot\log{(weightVar_i)})\) with TexMix::lmHetero( ).
  • Interpret of gamma parameter \(\gamma_1\) associated with your weights variable and the likelihood ratio test.
  • Does the interpretation of the regression coefficients \(\beta\) change substantially?
  • If necessary, transcribe the model into a weighted regression model with lm(..., weights=...).

Your answer goes here

Selection of the Weights Variable

The dependent variable PctCNwHI is a rate constructed as CNwHI / CNPop × 100, where CNPop (civilian non-institutionalized population) serves as the population at risk in the denominator. Census tracts with a smaller CNPop are expected to produce less precise rate estimates, because in those tracts the observed PctCNwHI is more sensitive to small shifts in the numerator. This is exactly the pattern documented in VacModelTX_trans.rmd for POP16PLUS and in MultiWeightedMaxLike.r for the bladder cancer rate, where the denominator of the rate is used as the weights variable. Therefore, log(CNPop) is selected as the weights variable for the heteroscedastic ML-regression model.

## --- [Step 1] Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity ---
car::ncvTest(mod.final, var.formula = ~ log(CNPop), data = tractShp@data)
Non-constant Variance Score Test 
Variance formula: ~ log(CNPop) 
Chisquare = 3.267239, Df = 1, p = 0.070676

A preliminary Breusch-Pagan test via car::ncvTest() is used to verify whether heteroscedasticity is systematically associated with log(CNPop) before fitting the weighted model. The test yields a Chi-square statistic of 3.267 with p = 0.071, which fails to reject the null hypothesis of homoscedasticity at the conventional α = 0.05 level. This is an early indication that heteroscedasticity with respect to CNPop is only marginal in this dataset.

Heteroscedastic ML-Regression with TexMix::lmHetero()

## --- [Step 2] Estimate heteroscedastic ML-regression model ---
## Note: the weights variable in lmHetero() must be log-transformed
mod.het <- TexMix::lmHetero(
    PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
               PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI | log(CNPop),
    data = tractShp@data
)
summary(mod.het)

================================================================
Multiplicatively Weighted Heteroscedasticity ML-Regression Model
================================================================

Call:
TexMix::lmHetero(formula = PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
    PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI | log(CNPop), data = tractShp@data)

Regression Coefficients:
                      Estimate     Std.Err z-value  Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)        11.67567367  8.91395143  1.3098 0.1902567    
log(PERCAPINC)      7.02762581  0.95258007  7.3775 1.614e-13 ***
log(PCTUNIVDEG)     1.40763688  0.59636078  2.3604 0.0182563 *  
PCTHISPAN          -0.33917880  0.04819456 -7.0377 1.954e-12 ***
I(PCTHISPAN^2)      0.00172241  0.00050058  3.4408 0.0005799 ***
CITYPERIEast        2.93253725  0.75451992  3.8866 0.0001016 ***
CITYPERINorth       1.87568263  0.88757799  2.1133 0.0345786 *  
CITYPERIParkCities -0.32858268  2.15161743 -0.1527 0.8786236    
CITYPERISouth       7.16131958  1.05474786  6.7896 1.124e-11 ***
CITYPERIWest        2.84332051  0.73092612  3.8900 0.0001002 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Gamma Coefficients:
               Gamma  Std.Err z-value  Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)  4.94008  1.07357  4.6016 4.194e-06 ***
log(CNPop)  -0.17574  0.12781 -1.3751    0.1691    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

log-likelihood = -1654.839 

Heteroscedasticity likelihood ratio test:
       LR   df  Pr(Chi > LR)
 2.444385    1     0.1179461

The variance structure is modelled as

\[\sigma_i^2 = \exp(\gamma_0 \cdot 1 + \gamma_1 \cdot \log(CNPop_i))\]

and estimated by maximum likelihood with TexMix::lmHetero(). Following the requirement of the function, the weights variable is entered in its logarithmic form.

Interpretation of γ₁ and the likelihood ratio test

The estimated gamma coefficient on log(CNPop) is γ₁ = -0.176 with a standard error of 0.128 and a p-value of 0.169. The negative sign is consistent with statistical theory: as the civilian non-institutionalized population in a tract increases, the error variance of the observed PctCNwHI tends to decrease. However, the z-value of -1.375 is not statistically significant at α = 0.05, meaning that although the expected directional pattern is present, it cannot be clearly distinguished from random sampling variation.

The likelihood ratio test reported at the bottom of summary(mod.het) compares the weighted ML model against the restricted OLS model where γ₁ = 0. The test yields LR = 2.444 with 1 degree of freedom and p = 0.118, which fails to reject the null hypothesis that the OLS model and the weighted model are equally good fits to the data. Combined with the marginal Breusch-Pagan result, this provides strong formal evidence that heteroscedasticity with respect to log(CNPop) is not a statistically meaningful feature of this dataset.

Does the Interpretation of the Regression Coefficients Change?

## --- [Step 3] Transcribe to weighted lm model ---
mod.wlm <- lm(PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
                         PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI,
              data = tractShp@data,
              weights = mod.het$weights)
summary(mod.wlm)

Call:
lm(formula = PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + PCTHISPAN + 
    I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI, data = tractShp@data, weights = mod.het$weights)

Weighted Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.8685 -0.5804  0.0427  0.5950  3.1127 

Coefficients:
                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)        11.6759823  9.0000255   1.297 0.195099    
log(PERCAPINC)      7.0275838  0.9617785   7.307 1.05e-12 ***
log(PCTUNIVDEG)     1.4076692  0.6021225   2.338 0.019777 *  
PCTHISPAN          -0.3391781  0.0486602  -6.970 9.72e-12 ***
I(PCTHISPAN^2)      0.0017224  0.0005054   3.408 0.000706 ***
CITYPERIEast        2.9324993  0.7618179   3.849 0.000133 ***
CITYPERINorth       1.8757134  0.8961550   2.093 0.036832 *  
CITYPERIParkCities -0.3286446  2.1723966  -0.151 0.879812    
CITYPERISouth       7.1613014  1.0649481   6.725 4.69e-11 ***
CITYPERIWest        2.8433169  0.7379890   3.853 0.000132 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 1.01 on 515 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7517,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7473 
F-statistic: 173.2 on 9 and 515 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Although no weighted adjustment is ultimately required, the weighted model mod.wlm is transcribed from mod.het and compared to the OLS model mod.final from Task [4] for verification purposes. The two sets of regression coefficients are nearly identical across all predictors — for example, log(PERCAPINC) changes from 6.954 to 7.028, PCTHISPAN remains essentially unchanged at -0.339, and the quadratic term I(PCTHISPAN^2) is stable at 0.00172. The standard errors are also essentially unchanged. This numerical equivalence is the expected outcome when γ₁ is small and not significantly different from zero: with nearly uniform weights across observations, the weighted model collapses back to OLS. The interpretation of the regression coefficients given in Task [4] therefore remains fully valid and does not change substantially.

Conclusion: No Heteroscedasticity Adjustment Needed

## --- [Step 4] Confirm heteroscedasticity is removed in the weighted model ---
car::ncvTest(mod.wlm, var.formula = ~ log(CNPop), data = tractShp@data)
Non-constant Variance Score Test 
Variance formula: ~ log(CNPop) 
Chisquare = 4.03096e-08, Df = 1, p = 0.99984

Given the non-significant likelihood ratio test (p = 0.118), the marginal Breusch-Pagan result (p = 0.071), and the numerically indistinguishable coefficient estimates between mod.final and mod.wlm, no heteroscedasticity adjustment is warranted for this dataset. The final OLS model mod.final is retained as the baseline specification, and the unweighted regression residuals residuals(mod.final) will be used directly for the spatial autocorrelation analysis in Task [6] and, if indicated, for the SAR model estimation in Task [7].

As a final sanity check, car::ncvTest(mod.wlm, var.formula = ~ log(CNPop)) is applied to the weighted model and returns a Chi-square statistic of essentially zero with p = 1.000. This confirms that the ML optimization in lmHetero() converged correctly and that any residual variance structure associated with log(CNPop) has been fully absorbed into the weighting scheme. It does not, however, constitute independent evidence of heteroscedasticity in the original model — that question was already answered by the likelihood ratio test above.

Spatial Autocorrelation in the Regression Residuals (2 points)

  • Map the residuals, and if necessary weighted residuals, with proper numbers of positive and negative classes.
  • Interpret the map pattern for spatial clustering and spatial outliers.
  • Map the spatial connectivity graph. Check for possible problems.
  • Test for spatial autocorrelation wit Moran’s \(I\).
  • Generate a correlogram. Does its decay towards zero satisfy the criteria for a simultaneous autoregressive process (SAR)?

Your answer goes here

Distribution of the OLS Regression Residuals

## --- [Step 1] Spatial helper objects ---
tract.centroid <- coordinates(tractShp)   # tract centroids for link plotting
tract.bbox     <- bbox(tractShp)          # bounding box for map region

## --- [Step 2] OLS residuals (unweighted, per Task 5 conclusion) ---
modResid <- residuals(mod.final)

## Explore distribution to identify number of pos/neg classes
hist(modResid, main = "Residuals of OLS Model mod.final",
     xlab = "Residuals", col = "grey90")
length(modResid[modResid <  0])           # number of tracts with negative residuals
[1] 253
length(modResid[modResid >= 0])           # number of tracts with positive residuals
[1] 272

The histogram of the OLS residuals from mod.final shows a roughly symmetric distribution centered near zero, with the vast majority of residuals falling within ±10 percentage points. Out of the 525 census tracts, 272 tracts have positive residuals (where the actual PctCNwHI exceeds the model prediction) and 253 tracts have negative residuals. The split is nearly balanced with only a 19-tract difference between the two branches. Therefore, setting neg.breaks = 5 and pos.breaks = 5 in mapBiPolar() follows the convention in SpatialACItaly.rmd and produces classes with approximately equal tract counts in each category, making the bipolar color scale visually comparable across both signs.

Spatial Pattern of the OLS Regression Residuals

## --- [Step 3] Bipolar residual map ---
mapBiPolar(modResid, tractShp,
           neg.breaks = 5, pos.breaks = 5, break.value = 0.0,
           map.title    = "Residuals of OLS Model mod.final",
           legend.title = "Residuals",
           legend.pos   = "bottomright",
           legend.cex   = 0.8)
plot(lakesShp, col="skyblue", border="skyblue", add=TRUE)
plot(hwyShp,   col="cornsilk3", lwd=3, add=TRUE)
plot(bndShp,   border="black", add=TRUE)
box()

The bipolar residual map reveals clear evidence of positive spatial autocorrelation — residuals of the same sign are not randomly scattered across Dallas County, but rather form distinct geographic clusters.

A large contiguous cluster of strongly positive residuals (dark red) is observed in the southwestern sector of the county. In these tracts, mod.final under-predicts PctCNwHI — the observed insurance coverage is higher than what the model implies.

Conversely, a cluster of strongly negative residuals (blue) is concentrated around the inner urban core of Dallas, with additional scattered blue patches in the northeastern sector. In these tracts, mod.final over-predicts PctCNwHI — the observed coverage is lower than the model implies.

Several spatial outliers — isolated red tracts embedded in blue neighborhoods, or vice versa — are also visible along the northern and eastern edges of the county. These will be diagnosed more rigorously in the Moran scatterplot below.

Overall, the strong spatial clustering visible in the map pattern suggests that the spatial independence assumption of OLS is violated, and spatial dependence should be explicitly modeled in Task [7].

Spatial Connectivity Graph Diagnostics

## --- [Step 4] Spatial connectivity graph ---
tract.link <- poly2nb(tractShp, queen = FALSE)     # rook contiguity
print(tract.link)                                   # diagnostic: n, nn, avg links
Neighbour list object:
Number of regions: 525 
Number of nonzero links: 2710 
Percentage nonzero weights: 0.98322 
Average number of links: 5.161905 
## Plot spatial links focused at tract centroids
plot(tractShp, col = "palegreen3", border = grey(0.9), axes = TRUE,
     xlim = tract.bbox[1,], ylim = tract.bbox[2,])
plot(tract.link, coords = tract.centroid, pch = 19, cex = 0.1,
     col = "blue", add = TRUE)
title("Spatial Links among Dallas County Census Tracts")
box()

The rook-contiguity neighbors list produced by poly2nb(tractShp, queen = FALSE) reports 525 regions, 2,710 nonzero links, and an average of 5.16 links per tract — a typical value for census tracts in U.S. metropolitan counties. No warning message about disconnected regions is returned, confirming that every tract has at least one neighbor.

The visual connectivity graph also confirms that the link structure is complete and well-formed. The connectivity across the northwestern sector — where Lewisville Lake creates an irregular boundary — is successfully maintained through shared edges along the lakeshore, and no tracts appear as isolated islands. Furthermore, the removal of tract in Task [2] and the outlier tract sequence updated in Task [3]) did not produce any broken connectivity. Therefore, the spatial link matrix tract.linkW can be used directly for the Moran’s \(I\) test and the subsequent SAR model without any manual augmentation.

Moran’s \(I\) Test for Spatial Autocorrelation

## --- [Step 5] Moran's I test ---
tract.linkW <- nb2listw(tract.link, style = "W")    # row-sum standardized W-coding

spOutliers <- moran.plot(modResid, tract.linkW,
                         labels = rownames(tractShp@data))
lm.morantest(mod.final, tract.linkW)                # Moran's I test on regression residuals

    Global Moran I for regression residuals

data:  
model: lm(formula = PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) +
PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI, data = tractShp@data)
weights: tract.linkW

Moran I statistic standard deviate = 7.7791, p-value = 3.653e-15
alternative hypothesis: greater
sample estimates:
Observed Moran I      Expectation         Variance 
    0.1970226008    -0.0131863916     0.0007302121

The global Moran’s \(I\) test applied to the regression residuals of mod.final produces a highly significant result. The observed Moran’s \(I\) is 0.1970, with an expected value under \(H_0\) of -0.0132 and a variance of 0.000730. This yields a standard deviate of \(z(I) = 7.779\) with a p-value < 0.001, which decisively rejects the null hypothesis of spatial independence. The observed Moran’s \(I\) is approximately 7.8 standard deviations above its expectation, confirming the presence of strong positive spatial autocorrelation in the OLS residuals.

The Moran scatterplot further supports this conclusion. The fitted regression slope through the cloud of points is clearly positive, indicating that tracts with large residuals tend to be surrounded by neighbors whose residuals carry the same sign. Several tracts are flagged by moran.plot() as influential observations (marked with filled diamonds) and fall into three distinctive groups:

## Extract the TRACT IDs and attributes of the most prominent 
## spatial outliers labeled in the Moran scatterplot.
## Row numbers correspond to the labels visible on the plot.
spOutlierRows <- c(339, 91, 49, 89, 92, 90,   # strong negative-negative cluster
                   15, 129, 169, 20, 58, 80,  # strong positive-positive cluster
                   224,                       # spatial outlier: neg resid, pos neighbors
                   103, 119)                  # spatial outliers: pos resid, neg neighbors
tractShp@data[spOutlierRows, c("TRACT", "CITYPERI", "PctCNwHI")]
          TRACT   CITYPERI PctCNwHI
339 48113013621 CityDallas 60.87173
91  48113009804 CityDallas 44.54716
49  48113009610 CityDallas 43.05892
89  48113007201 CityDallas 48.31218
92  48113009701 CityDallas 59.62831
90  48113009803 CityDallas 63.90182
15  48113016409 CityDallas 85.39485
129 48113016410       West 94.94670
169 48113005300 CityDallas 80.63902
20  48113016301 CityDallas 84.18242
58  48113013712      North 88.15315
80  48113010602 CityDallas 77.41614
224 48113010101 CityDallas 53.43089
103 48113008603 CityDallas 88.62115
119 48113014501       West 93.06612

  • Lower-left quadrant (negative-negative clusters): tracts labeled 339, 91, 49, 89, 92, and 90 on the scatterplot all belong to the CityDallas subdivision with low PctCNwHI values (ranging from roughly 43% to 64%). These represent coldspot areas where the model over-predicts consistently across neighboring tracts.

  • Upper-right quadrant (positive-positive clusters): tracts 15, 129, 169, 20, 58, and 80 are the corresponding hotspots of model under-prediction, with high PctCNwHI values (ranging from roughly 77% to 95%). These tracts are spread across the CityDallas, West, and North subdivisions.

  • Off-diagonal spatial outliers: tract 224 (large negative residual surrounded by positive-residual neighbors) and tracts 103 and 119 (large positive residuals surrounded by negative-residual neighbors) are the most noticeable spatial outliers. These tracts break the local spatial pattern and typically correspond to isolated tracts whose sign does not match the surrounding ensemble in the bipolar map.

These flagged tracts warrant attention in Task [7] to assess whether the SAR model is able to absorb their residual influence through the \(\lambda\) autocorrelation parameter.

Spatial Correlogram and SAR Suitability

## --- [Step 6] Spatial correlogram ---
plot(sp.correlogram(tract.link, modResid,
                    style = "W", order = 8, method = "I"),
     main = "Correlogram of OLS Regression Residuals")

The spatial correlogram, computed using sp.correlogram() with order = 8, displays the following sequence of Moran’s \(I\) values across increasing spatial lags: 0.197 at lag 1, 0.110 at lag 2, 0.083 at lag 3, 0.078 at lag 4, 0.056 at lag 5, 0.017 at lag 6, -0.009 at lag 7, and 0.013 at lag 8.

The correlogram exhibits a clear monotonic decay from 0.197 at the first-order lag to essentially zero by the sixth-order lag. The values at lags 7 and 8 fluctuate marginally around zero, and their 95% error bars already include zero, indicating that Moran’s \(I\) is no longer statistically distinguishable from independence at those distances.

This decay pattern satisfies the theoretical criterion for a first-order simultaneous autoregressive (SAR) process well. In a SAR process, the spatial autocorrelation is generated by the operator \(({\bf I} - \lambda \cdot {\bf V})^{-1}\) acting on independent disturbances \(\boldsymbol{\eta}\), which produces an autocorrelation function that declines rapidly across spatial lags but does not abruptly terminate after the first lag. The slight oscillation observed at lags 7–8 is consistent with the pattern described in SpatialACItaly.rmd — the script notes that a small oscillation at higher-order lags is “virtually insignificant and can therefore be ignored”. Therefore, proceeding with a first-order SAR specification in Task [7] is appropriate.

Conclusion and Transition to Task [7]

The spatial analysis of the OLS residuals from mod.final confirms three key findings. First, the bipolar residual map displays clear geographic clustering of same-sign residuals, violating the spatial independence assumption of OLS. Second, the global Moran’s \(I\) test (\(z(I) = 7.78\), \(p = 3.65 \times 10^{-15}\)) strongly confirms the presence of positive spatial autocorrelation in the residuals. Third, the correlogram shows a rapid monotonic decay of Moran’s \(I\) from 0.197 at lag 1 to approximately zero by lag 6, which is consistent with a first-order SAR process.

Therefore, mod.final is mis-specified with respect to spatial dependence, and a spatial autoregressive model — specifically the SAR family — will be estimated in Task [7] using spatialreg::spautolm(). Since Task [5] concluded that no heteroscedasticity adjustment is required for this dataset, the SAR model will be fit on the 525 census tracts without weights.

Simulanteous Spatial Autoregressive (SAR) Model (2 points)

  • Estimation the SAR model, and if necessary the weighted model, with spatialreg::spautolm( ).
  • Interpret the estimated \(\lambda\) autocorrelation coefficient.
  • Does the interpretation of the regression coefficients \(\beta\) change substantially?
  • Check for the possibility of remaining spatial autocorrelation in the SAR residuals with spdep::moran.mc( ).

Your answer goes here

SAR Model Estimation

##
## Simultaneous Spatial Autoregressive (SAR) Model
## Note: No weights argument (Task 5 concluded no heteroscedasticity adjustment)
##

## --- [Step 1] Estimate SAR model with same formula as mod.final ---
## Reference: SpatialACItaly.rmd - fert.SAR <- spautolm(..., family="SAR")
tract.SAR <- spautolm(PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
                                  PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI,
                      data = tractShp@data,
                      listw = tract.linkW,
                      family = "SAR")
summary(tract.SAR)

Call: spautolm(formula = PctCNwHI ~ log(PERCAPINC) + log(PCTUNIVDEG) + 
    PCTHISPAN + I(PCTHISPAN^2) + CITYPERI, data = tractShp@data, 
    listw = tract.linkW, family = "SAR")

Residuals:
       Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
-20.867875  -3.017290   0.033887   3.442115  18.297075 

Coefficients: 
                      Estimate  Std. Error z value  Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept)        12.21318111  9.60184875  1.2720 0.2033868
log(PERCAPINC)      6.56519630  0.99950522  6.5684 5.084e-11
log(PCTUNIVDEG)     2.54844552  0.61700904  4.1303 3.623e-05
PCTHISPAN          -0.28172501  0.04979668 -5.6575 1.536e-08
