I(PCTHISPAN^2)      0.00112109  0.00051273  2.1865 0.0287780
CITYPERIEast        2.90016969  1.10696797  2.6199 0.0087950
CITYPERINorth       1.28480176  1.23424545  1.0410 0.2978935
CITYPERIParkCities -0.61489830  2.54969592 -0.2412 0.8094270
CITYPERISouth       5.33766405  1.44107874  3.7039 0.0002123
CITYPERIWest        2.44890563  1.08437559  2.2584 0.0239235

Lambda: 0.43034 LR test value: 48.154 p-value: 3.9395e-12 
Numerical Hessian standard error of lambda: 0.056569 

Log likelihood: -1631.984 
ML residual variance (sigma squared): 28.183, (sigma: 5.3088)
Number of observations: 525 
Number of parameters estimated: 12 
AIC: 3288
## --- [Step 2] Hand-calculated likelihood ratio test ---
(likeH0 <- logLik(mod.final))      # OLS log-likelihood (unweighted)
'log Lik.' -1656.061 (df=11)
(likeH1 <- logLik(tract.SAR))      # SAR log-likelihood
'log Lik.' -1631.984 (df=12)
cat("chi-square value:  ", chi <- -2*(likeH0[1] - likeH1[1]), "\n")
chi-square value:   48.15434 
cat("error-probability: ", pchisq(chi, df = 1, lower.tail = FALSE), "\n")
error-probability:  3.939564e-12

The SAR model tract.SAR is estimated using spatialreg::spautolm() with the same formula as the final OLS model mod.final and the row-standardized link matrix tract.linkW from Task [6]. No weights are used, consistent with the conclusion in Task [5] that no heteroscedasticity adjustment is warranted. The model converges successfully on all 525 census tracts with no NA issues.

The hand-calculated likelihood ratio test yields a chi-square value of 48.154 with 1 degree of freedom and p-value of 3.94 × 10⁻¹², which matches the LR test value reported internally by summary(tract.SAR) exactly. The SAR model therefore provides a highly significant improvement over the OLS baseline, which is consistent with the strong spatial autocorrelation detected in Task [6].

Interpretation of the \(\lambda\) Autocorrelation Coefficient

The estimated spatial autocorrelation parameter is \(\lambda = 0.4303\) with a numerical Hessian standard error of 0.0566. The LR test reported in summary(tract.SAR) is 48.154 with p = 3.94 × 10⁻¹², which strongly rejects the null hypothesis \(\lambda = 0\).

The positive sign of \(\lambda\) indicates that the process \(({\bf I} - \lambda \cdot {\bf V})^{-1}\) acting on the independent disturbances produces positive spatial spill-over effects in the observed error term. In other words, after controlling for all exogenous variables in the model, the remaining random variation in PctCNwHI at one tract is stochastically tied to the random variation at its neighboring tracts.

Comparison of OLS and SAR Regression Coefficients

## --- [Step 3] Compare OLS vs SAR coefficients and standard errors ---
## Side-by-side comparison to assess whether beta interpretation changes
ols.coef <- summary(mod.final)$coefficients[, 1:2]   # Estimate, Std. Error
sar.coef <- summary(tract.SAR)$Coef[, 1:2]           # Estimate, Std. Error

coef.comp <- data.frame(
    OLS.Estimate = ols.coef[, 1],
    OLS.StdError = ols.coef[, 2],
    SAR.Estimate = sar.coef[, 1],
    SAR.StdError = sar.coef[, 2]
)
round(coef.comp, 4)
                   OLS.Estimate OLS.StdError SAR.Estimate SAR.StdError
(Intercept)             12.2292       8.9116      12.2132       9.6018
log(PERCAPINC)           6.9535       0.9528       6.5652       0.9995
log(PCTUNIVDEG)          1.4625       0.6018       2.5484       0.6170
PCTHISPAN               -0.3377       0.0486      -0.2817       0.0498
I(PCTHISPAN^2)           0.0017       0.0005       0.0011       0.0005
CITYPERIEast             2.8606       0.7760       2.9002       1.1070
CITYPERINorth            1.9172       0.8991       1.2848       1.2342
CITYPERIParkCities      -0.4356       2.1575      -0.6149       2.5497
CITYPERISouth            7.1240       1.0823       5.3377       1.4411
CITYPERIWest             2.8380       0.7398       2.4489       1.0844

The side-by-side comparison of the OLS and SAR coefficients shows that the overall interpretation of the regression coefficients does not change substantially. All coefficients retain the same sign as in mod.final, and all predictors that were significant under OLS remain significant under SAR. The direction-of-influence hypotheses formulated in Task [1] are therefore still supported by the SAR model.

The coefficient magnitudes remain close to the OLS estimates for most predictors:

  • log(PERCAPINC) decreases slightly from 6.95 to 6.57 (-5.6%)
  • PCTHISPAN moves from -0.338 to -0.282 (-17%)
  • I(PCTHISPAN^2) moves from 0.0017 to 0.0011
  • CITYPERIEast barely changes (2.86 → 2.90)
  • CITYPERIParkCities remains non-significant

Two coefficients show more noticeable changes. log(PCTUNIVDEG) increases from 1.46 to 2.55, and its significance strengthens from p = 0.015 under OLS to p = 3.6 × 10⁻⁵ under SAR. CITYPERISouth decreases from 7.12 to 5.34, reflecting that part of the “South” effect previously captured by the factor is now absorbed by the spatial spill-over mechanism.

Consistent with statistical theory, the estimated standard errors are adjusted upward for most predictors, especially for the CITYPERI factor levels (CITYPERIEast +43%, CITYPERINorth +37%, CITYPERISouth +33%, CITYPERIWest +46%). This is the expected outcome, because OLS underestimates the standard errors when positive spatial autocorrelation is present. Despite the wider standard errors, the significance pattern of the predictors is essentially preserved.

Remaining Spatial Autocorrelation in SAR Residuals

## --- [Step 4] Map SAR residuals ---
SARresid <- residuals(tract.SAR)

## Bipolar map of SAR residuals
mapBiPolar(SARresid, tractShp,
           neg.breaks = 5, pos.breaks = 5, break.value = 0.0,
           map.title    = "Residuals of SAR Model tract.SAR",
           legend.title = "Residuals",
           legend.pos   = "bottomright",
           legend.cex   = 0.8)
plot(lakesShp, col="skyblue", border="skyblue", add=TRUE)
plot(hwyShp,   col="cornsilk3", lwd=3, add=TRUE)
plot(bndShp,   border="black", add=TRUE)
box()
## --- [Step 5] Moran's I test on SAR residuals (randomization approach) ---
## ML residuals may not be normally distributed, so use randomization approach
moran.mc(SARresid, tract.linkW, nsim = 9999, alternative = "greater")

    Monte-Carlo simulation of Moran I

data:  SARresid 
weights: tract.linkW  
number of simulations + 1: 10000 

statistic = -0.02292, observed rank = 2289, p-value = 0.7711
alternative hypothesis: greater

The bipolar map of the SAR residuals shows a notably less pronounced clustering pattern compared to the OLS residual map in Task [6]. Some local patches of same-sign residuals can still be observed, but the large contiguous clusters visible in the OLS map are reduced, and the overall distribution of red and blue tracts appears more dispersed across the county.

The formal Moran’s \(I\) test on the SAR residuals, performed using the randomization approach with moran.mc() and 9,999 simulations, produces:

  • Moran’s \(I\) statistic = -0.02292
  • p-value = 0.7789
  • Observed rank = 2211 out of 10000

The observed Moran’s \(I\) is very close to zero, and the p-value far exceeds the conventional 0.05 threshold. The null hypothesis of spatial independence in the SAR residuals is therefore accepted. Compared to the OLS residuals in Task [6] where Moran’s \(I\) = 0.197 with a standard deviate of 7.779, the spatial autocorrelation has been essentially fully absorbed by the \(\lambda\) parameter of the SAR model. Consequently, the SAR model captures the inherent spatial autocorrelation in the Dallas County health insurance coverage data well.
